name: Data Structures
author: Recurse
description: Lists, dicts, sets, tuples, and tradeoffs.

questions.question: Which structure is best for fast membership checks?
questions.explanation: Sets are optimized for membership tests.

questions.question: Fill the blank to remove and return the last stack item.
questions.explanation: pop() removes the last item and returns it.

questions.question: What is the problem here?
choices: A set is a better choice when you only need membership tests
in is not allowed in Python
The variable x must be a string
questions.explanation: A set makes the membership test clearer and faster for this use case.

questions.question: Which is immutable?
questions.explanation: Tuples cannot be changed in place.

sections.title: Pick the right structure
sections.body: A data structure is a decision about how you want to organize and access data. The right choice depends on whether you care about ordering, uniqueness, random access, or fast lookup.
sections.tip: Choose the structure that makes the operation cheap.

sections.title: Lists and tuples
sections.body: Lists are mutable ordered sequences. Tuples are ordered too, but they are immutable. Use tuples when the data should not change.
sections.tip: If the collection should not be edited, tuple is often the cleaner choice.

sections.title: Sets and dictionaries
sections.body: Sets are for membership and uniqueness. Dictionaries are for key-value lookups. Both are hash-based, which is why membership checks are usually fast.
sections.tip: Use a dict when you need to map keys to values.

sections.title: Stacks, queues, and Big O
sections.body: A stack is LIFO. A queue is FIFO. Big O tells you how runtime or memory grows as the input size grows, which helps you compare tradeoffs.
sections.tip: Focus on growth, not exact milliseconds.

keyTerms.definition: An unordered collection of unique values.
A way to describe how cost grows with input size.

lesson.feynmanPrompt: Explain why you would choose a set over a list when checking if an item already exists.
